title: Sculptural bird in paper clay
subtitle: A solid hand-modelled bird with slab wings and a pinched body
excerpt: Paper clay is air-dry clay reinforced with cellulose fibres, which makes it stronger than standard air-dry clay when thin sections are joined. A sculptural bird uses a solid pinched body, slab tail and wings joined at leather-hard, and a needle-tool beak. The fibre reinforcement stops the thin wing edges from cracking. No kiln or wheel is needed.
sourceNotes: Sculptural hand-building technique in paper clay. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: Air-dry clay mixed with cellulose fibres. Stronger than standard air-dry clay when dry and more forgiving at thin edges and joins.
The drying stage where the body is stiff but still slightly damp, the right point for attaching wings and tail without distorting the body.
Scratching joining surfaces and applying clay slurry before pressing together. Use paper-clay slip for paper-clay joins.

content.text: is the better choice for a sculptural bird over standard air-dry clay because thin sections, beak, wing edges, tail tip, stay intact rather than cracking when they dry. The fibre reinforcement is not visible in the finished piece; it just makes fragile edges stronger. The bird is built in two sessions: body on day one, appendages at
on day two. No kiln or wheel is needed.

attrs.title: Paper clay slip
attrs.body: When joining paper clay to paper clay, use slip made from the same paper clay, dissolve offcuts in water until they reach a thick cream consistency. Ordinary water-clay slip works but the fibre content of paper-clay slip gives a stronger join at thin edges.

items.name: Pottery rolling pin and 5 mm guide rails

content.text: Roll 250 g of paper clay into an oval about 10 cm long and 6 cm wide, a rounded teardrop shape, wider at one end (the breast) and narrower at the other (where the tail will attach). Compress the surface with the wooden rib to close any cracks. Set on a canvas board resting on its flat underside.
Roll 50 g into a ball 4 cm across.

the junction between body and head. Press the head onto the wide end of the body and blend the neck join smoothly with a damp finger, working the clay in from both sides until the seam disappears. Leave overnight under loose plastic wrap.

Roll a slab 5 mm thick. Cut two wing shapes, a rounded triangle about 8 cm long and 4 cm at the base. Score matching areas on both sides of the body and on the flat face of each wing. Apply slip and press the wings on with the flat face against the body, angled slightly back. Support each wing with a small ball of scrunched plastic wrap underneath until the join firms.

Cut a fan-shaped tail 6 cm wide and 3 cm long from the same slab. Score and slip the narrow end of the tail and the rear of the body. Press together and blend. For the beak, roll a small cone of clay 15 mm long. Score and slip the tip of the head at the midpoint of the face. Press the beak on and blend at its base. Pierce two small holes with the needle tool for eyes.
Dry bone-dry over three to four days, away from direct heat. Sand any rough edges with 120-grit sandpaper. Seal with two coats of PVA diluted 1:1 before painting with acrylics.
Carve feather lines into the wings with the needle tool before they dry for textured detail.
Make a pair or a flock of birds at different sizes for a grouped shelf display.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Problems when building the sculptural bird.

items.symptom: Wings crack away at the join as the piece dries
items.cause: Wings attached to a fully dry body, or insufficient scoring.
items.fix: Attach wings at leather-hard, not bone-dry. Score six to eight hatch lines on each surface. Apply generous slip and hold in position for 30 seconds before releasing.

items.cause: Beak not blended into the head sufficiently at the base.
items.fix: Build up a small collar of fresh clay around the beak base and blend it into the head. Paper clay patch sticks even to dry clay if slip is applied first.
